speaker: interviewer
text: The--the Red Cross didn't come?

speaker: interviewer
text: OK.

speaker: interviewer
text: Interview with Rose Kaplovitz on January-- sorry, on April 3, 1995. Tape five. [PAUSES FOR 8 SECONDS] Tell me more about what happened immediately after the liberation.

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, as I said, the first impact, of course, is to want to go back home. But the transportation of Europe was totally paralyzed. So we couldn't immediately return home. So we had to stay for close to two weeks longer. And then finally, we made it safely home. Safely-- not so safely, because we had to deal with the Russians also. But anyway, eventually, we arrived in Sosnowiec.

speaker: interviewer
text: Who is we?

speaker: interviewee
text: My two sisters and I. And it was then-- that this was the worst day in my life. If ever, ever, ever I should have contemplated suicide-- and I never did-- this should have been the day. It was a day I shall never forget. We came home, hoping to be reunited with our families. We came back to the place where we use to live. Strangers were living there. Yes, our furniture was there. But the faces of the people were strange. It was then that we discovered that my mother, my father, my sister Regina, her five-year-old little girl, Lola, my younger sister, [NON-ENGLISH] have all perished in Auschwitz. My brother was already dead six years. It was then that we discovered we came back to nothing. There was a Jewish committee set up. And they tried to give us a dwelling, like, one room. And by that time, we found my older sister, who lived in Kielce, who had a son who was born on January 1, 1940, who survived, only by the good heart of two Christian families. We are so proud of him being alive. We are so proud of the two families, who risked their life to save him. He's today a professor at the University of Massachusetts. And he does write books. He's a scientist and physicist and mathematician, extremely bright. And my sister also survived. Her husband did not. We stayed in Poland. My sisters wanted me to go back to school, since they didn't know what to do with me. But I refused to go to back to school and be the only Jewish child in the school.

speaker: interviewer
text: So I was sent to an orphanage in a nearby town, in Chrzanow-- Chorzow-- where there was an orphanage open for children who survived in Christian homes or concentration camps, like myself. Because not only did we have to live there, but we also had to go to school there. It was all in the same building.

speaker: interviewee
text: But I stayed there just for a short time. Seeing that there is no future left for us in Poland, we decided to leave. I left Poland. And oddly enough, I had to leave Poland undercover, as a Greek citizen returning home. I don't know why we could not just openly leave and go wherever we wanted. I arrived in Germany. And oddly enough, we had to go and live in DP camps, displaced person camps

speaker: interviewer
text: How did you make that journey, by train?

speaker: interviewee
text: By different trains.

speaker: interviewer
text: How did you get money?

speaker: interviewee
text: Who had money? I didn't have money.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you buy a train ticket?

speaker: interviewee
text: I didn't buy a train ticket. I went with a group. They smuggled us.

speaker: interviewer
text: Oh.

speaker: interviewee
text: And I had no money. I didn't have money. I never had a coin in my hand, until I came to the United States. So I didn't know what money is by that time. I arrived in Leipheim. And since all of us, my sisters and I, couldn't leave at the same time, we left at different times. And since I was the youngest, they wanted me first out of the country. And I came to Leipheim DP camp, where I joined what was called then a 'kibbutz.' That was a place where we were preparing ourselves for the life in Israel. I lived there for a while. And I so longed to go to Israel. I learned all the songs. I learned all about Palestine. And I longed to go there and be in a place of my own. But the head of the illegal aliyah, of the people that were smuggling us to Palestine at that time-- he found out that I have older sisters who wished to go to America because we had an uncle here. He, who was in charge of smuggling people out of Germany to Israel, came over to me and sat down with me and tried to talk to me. And this is what he said to me. Listen, Rosie, you are a very young girl. I know you would like to go to Israel. I know we would like you to come. We want every Jew there. But I found out that you have older sisters. And if you should come to Palestine, you might be very lonely, you might be very sad. You might miss your family. And you might be a very unhappy girl. It is my duty to see to it that you go with your older sisters. And if you ever decide to come to Palestine, remember, we will always let you in. And his wisdom directed me to go together with my sisters. But because I was still a teenager-- and when I registered for the United States, as a teenager, I did not have to wait for the quota. And therefore, I was sent immediately in 1947, to the United States. My sisters, because of whom I came to America, did not arrive here until '49 and '50. I cannot describe to you the feeling I had when I arrived to the shores of the United States, on a battleship on September 21, 1947. I cannot tell you what that meant to me and to those on the boat. It spelled freedom. It spelled liberty. It spelled everything that it represented. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I cannot tell you the feeling that I had that I am in the country where I will be free, where I don't have to live in fear. I cannot describe to you the feeling I had when I walked through the streets of New York, free, able to look, able to stop, able to talk to people. I stayed in New York for several weeks--

speaker: interviewer
text: I must ask you, how-- had you made contact with this uncle?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, my sisters did. I really did not, wasn't anxious, because my aim was to go to Palestine.

speaker: interviewer
text: And did he--

speaker: interviewee
text: And my-- when I came here, my uncle requested-- I came, and I stayed in a children's home in New York. And my uncle requested that I be sent to him. And I did. I feared to go there a little bit, because he also had two teenage daughters. And I was worried how we would get along. But we did. When I came to Cleveland, I was put under the jurisdiction of the Jewish Children's Bureau. I was sent back to school. I was sent first to a class where we just learned basic English, to be able to just get along.

speaker: interviewer
text: And then they had to place me in a class. How do you place a 17-year-old child who has not been eight years to school, who doesn't know the language? Well, I finally was placed in the ninth grade.

speaker: interviewee
text: But the date that I entered school, I have made up my mind. I made the decision that because my education was taken away from me by force, because I was robbed of something that every normal child should have a privilege to have, I decided that day that I shall recapture all of it, in spite of everything, even in spite of the barrier of not knowing the language. And I threw myself with my whole being into recapturing that bit of education that I needed, in order to become a decent and normal human being. I am grateful to all my teachers who were helpful. I'm grateful to America and her people, for giving me opportunity for a new life. I went through school. The students were fantastic. I didn't see the prejudice that I see today in school. They were helpful. They were teaching me. Usually when they were laughing because of my language, they were not really laughing at me, but rather, with me. They made me feel very comfortable. My homeroom teacher, Miss Schleger, who was of German descent, was most fantastic to me. She tried to teach me ways to become a teenager. She kept on saying, you're much too old for your age. And she did such wonderful thing for me-- and all my teachers. I loved school. And because I was so determined, because I wanted to learn, I was fortunate enough to be able to graduate from high school. I graduated with honors. I was on the honor roll. I graduated the fifth highest in my graduating class at John Adams High School. I was offered a scholarship to a university, which at times, I regret that I didn't take advantage. But you must understand, I was in this country already for several years. I was a little Orphan Annie. And I felt as such. My clothes were supplied by the Jewish Children's Bureau. My allowance was given also by them. And I finally wanted to have a home of my own. I also have met Henry, my husband. And I wanted to get married and have a place that I could call my own. I hadn't had a real, decent home since 1939. And this was the time for me. I-- Therefore, instead of taking advantage of the scholarship offered me, I got married. I never regretted that. Eventually, I went back to school. And eventually, I went to the College of Jewish Studies-- was going there for 13 years. Because when I was in high school, I went to my social worker after three months. And I told her, I want to go to Hebrew school.

speaker: interviewer
text: And she looked at me in amazement. And she said, what? Are you out of your mind? You haven't been to school for eight years.

speaker: interviewee
text: You have a lot of catching up to do in a language that you don't know. And now you want Hebrew. Explain to me why. And I did. I told her that I know that English I will learn, because I must. But Hebrew I need to learn because I am a Jew. And I suffered only because I am a Jew. I lost my family, my home. And yet, I know nothing of my tradition, of my history, of my religion. I don't know how to pray. And I told her, this I need to be a human being, this I needed for my own person. And they let me go to Hebrew school. I went four times a week, after school. And I graduated together, at the same time when I graduated from high school. Then, when my children were born, and I married my husband, whom I was married for 41 years. And he died two years ago. I raised a wonderful family. I also went to the College of Jewish Studies, where I became a teacher of Hebrew and Jewish history. And I have been speaking on the Holocaust since 1970, trying to educate people, young people especially, teaching them about prejudice, but also about compassion. This is the only way I feel I can give some of what I received from this country, that I can give some of it back. I'm glad that I am able to do it. Many survivors are very-- have difficulty opening up and expressing themselves because it's too painful. I hope I can continue doing it for as long as I can. And I hope through educating young people, they can learn about it also and stop hating and start understanding each other. Only then can we have a better world, in which they-- my children and my grandchildren-- can live in peace. If not, I don't know where-- what direction we are going to. I hope this tape and those of others will teach people what can happen when people lose control, when people don't care about others. And we never know-- this time it was me-- we don't know who will be a murderer, who will be the victim, and who will the silent bystander. Today, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I blame the Nazis for what happened to me. But I also blame the people of the free world, who stood by silently and allowed this to happen. Nobody can tell me they did not know. Because if you pick up any paper-- from between 1942 till now-- you will see, there were constantly articles. The knowledge was there. Only people did not know because they did not choose to know. We cannot stand by when others are suffering. And unless we do something about it, this world will not be for our children, either safe. So I hope we all learn from these tapes that many survivors, to whom this is very painful, are providing for the generations to come, when we no longer will be here, to share our stories with them. Thank you.

speaker: interviewer
text: Thank you.

speaker: interviewee
text: This is a picture taken in 1938, in my school. It was during a play for Mother's Day. That's the only picture I have of my mother. The lady at whom there is an arrow above her head, pointing at her, that's my mother. That's the only picture I have of her. This is a picture of my father, Mendel Zaks, was taken beginning of the war, 1940. That's also the only picture I have of my beloved father, whom I shall never, never forget. This is my brother, Romek, who was killed by the Nazis the very first day of the war. He was barely 15 years old. And him too, I shall never forget. This picture is of me. I am on the left side. And my sister, [NON-ENGLISH], my younger sister, is on the right side. This was taken in our backyard. And the boy is a neighbor. I think his name was Abram, but I am not sure that I remember. But we played together. This picture, on the left side are two of my best girlfriends, Ruth Bachman and [NON-ENGLISH] [NON-ENGLISH]. And then I am next to them. And my sister, [NON-ENGLISH] is next to me. And in front of all of us is my little niece, my sister Regina's little daughter, Lola. My sister and my little niece and both of my girlfriends have all perished in Auschwitz.

speaker: interviewer
text: You should say the year-- this was?

speaker: interviewee
text: Mm-hmm.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: This is a picture of me, already during the war. It was taken in either end of 1940-- 1940, in Poland, during the war already, beginning of the war. This is a school picture taken in 1938. It was for mothers. They-- we put up a play in school. And I am the one on the right side, next to the girl dressed as a boy. It was a picture in which we performed for our parents. And there is my teacher, Helen Bergman, playing on the piano. I treasure these pictures with all my life. This is a picture of Henry Kaplovitz, at the age of 19, when he arrived in the United States in 1947. He, too, was a survivor. We were married in 1951. He was in such concentration camp as Auschwitz, Mauthausen, Ebensee, and [NON-ENGLISH]. And he passed away two years ago, to my sorrow. This is a picture of my family. This is the last picture taken together with my husband. It was taken in 1992. And starting from the right, that's my husband, Henry Kaplovitz. Next to him, it's-- I am. Next is my daughter-in-law, Marla. And my-- next to her is my son, Ronnie. Below them is my son, Mark. Next to him is my daughter-in-law, Elise. And next is my daughter, Heddy, and her husband, Fred Gallen. In front of them are my four grandchildren. My fifth one wasn't born yet. From the right, the oldest one is Jenna Gallen and her sister, Katie Gallen. Then it's Joshua Kaplovitz and his sister, Bree Kaplovitz. And I love them all dearly. And this is the latest addition to my family. This is my grandson, born to my son, Ronnie and Marla. And his name is David Henry Kaplovitz. And he carries my husband's name. In Hebrew, he is [HEBREW]. And I love him. And I wish my husband would have seen him. This is a picture of myself with my five grandchildren. Starting from the right, it's Jenna, then it's Katie, then myself. And next to me is Joshua. And on the floor is Bree. And in my hands is the newest baby in our family, David. I hope they will be able to carry on the tradition. And I am so proud of them. And I hope, wherever my husband is, he watches over them and he, too, is proud of them. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] May God bless them all.
